text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology
Pancreatic Cancer Program

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Medical Oncology Attending]
VISIT TYPE: Post-operative follow-up / Adjuvant therapy planning

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Margaret Osei-Bonsu is a 67-year-old female with a history of borderline resectable pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the head and uncinate process, now approximately 10 weeks following open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy (Whipple procedure) with en bloc SMV resection and reconstruction, presenting for post-operative oncology follow-up and discussion of adjuvant systemic chemotherapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Osei-Bonsu presented initially approximately 18 months ago with progressive painless jaundice, significant unintentional weight loss (14 lbs), new-onset epigastric pain radiating to the back, and pale, greasy stools. Workup at that time revealed obstructive jaundice with bilirubin peaking at 8.2 mg/dL, markedly elevated CA 19-9 at 1,204 U/mL, and a 3.8 cm hypoenhancing mass in the head and uncinate process of the pancreas on CT with pancreatic protocol. The mass demonstrated SMA abutment over approximately 170-180 degrees of circumference and SMV involvement with focal narrowing, meeting criteria for borderline resectable disease. EUS-guided biopsy confirmed moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma. She was staged as cT3 N1 M0 (Stage IIB, AJCC 8th edition).

Given borderline resectable status, she was taken to multidisciplinary tumor board, which recommended neoadjuvant therapy. Prior to initiating chemotherapy, ERCP with placement of a fully covered self-expanding metallic biliary stent (FCSEMS) was performed without complication, with bilirubin normalizing to 1.8 mg/dL within two weeks.

She proceeded with neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX (oxali 85 mg/m2, iri 150 mg/m2, leucovorin 400 mg/m2, 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 continuous infusion over 46 hours) every 14 days with Neulasta prophylaxis. She completed 6 cycles of full FOLFIRINOX; however, she developed progressive peripheral sensory neuropathy that reached grade 3 (interfering with ADLs) by cycle 6, necessitating discontinuation of Eloxatin. She completed cycles 7 and 8 with FOLFIRI backbone only (Camptosar, leucovorin, 5-FU). Over the course of neoadjuvant chemotherapy, her CA 19-9 declined from 1,204 to 85 U/mL, and restaging imaging after 8 cycles demonstrated the primary mass had decreased from 3.8 cm to 2.6 cm, with SMA abutment reduced to less than 90 degrees and SMV patency restored.

Following tumor board review, she underwent consolidation chemoradiation — IMRT with simultaneous integrated boost to the gross tumor volume and regional nodes (54 Gy in 28 fractions) with concurrent cape 825 mg/m2 twice daily on radiation days as a radiosensitizer. She completed chemoradiation over approximately 5.5 weeks. Pre-surgical re-staging CT showed the mass reduced to 2.3 cm with SMA abutment now less than 60 degrees, and CA 19-9 was 52 U/mL. PET-CT confirmed no FDG-avid distant metastases.

She subsequently underwent open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with en bloc segmental SMV resection and end-to-end venous reconstruction. The operation required 7 hours and 40 minutes, with estimated blood loss of 820 mL and receipt of 2 units of packed red blood cells intraoperatively. Intraoperative frozen sections of the pancreatic neck, common bile duct, and vascular margins were negative. Final pathology demonstrated moderately differentiated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, 2.1 cm, with moderate treatment effect (CAP tumor regression grade 2). Margins were all negative on permanent sections: pancreatic neck margin with 3 mm clearance, SMA/retroperitoneal margin with 1.2 mm clearance, SMV margin with 2 mm clearance. One of 22 sampled lymph nodes contained metastatic carcinoma. Perineural invasion was identified. Final pathologic stage: ypT3 N1 M0 (Stage IIB), R0 resection.

NGS on the resected specimen (648-gene panel) revealed KRAS p.G12V, TP53 p.R175H, SMAD4 homozygous deletion, and CDKN2A/p16 homozygous deletion. Tumor mutational burden was low (3 mutations/Mb). Microsatellite stable. No BRCA1/2, ATM, or PALB2 pathogenic mutations. No actionable targets identified. Germline testing was confirmatory for BRCA1/2 wild-type.

Her post-operative course was complicated by a Grade B pancreatic fistula (drain amylase 4,280 U/L on POD 3). This required NPO status, total parenteral nutrition, octreotide 200 mcg subcutaneously three times daily, and prolonged drain maintenance. She was discharged home on POD 14 with a Jackson-Pratt drain in situ. The drain was removed approximately 6 weeks post-surgery following sinogram confirming fistula closure. She presents today, approximately 10 weeks post-surgery, with the drain removed for 2 weeks. She reports progressive improvement in overall energy and appetite. She denies nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea. She endorses persistent tingling and numbness in the fingertips bilaterally, which she attributes to prior oxali, unchanged from prior assessments. She reports her stools have improved with institution of pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy. She does note that her blood sugars have been more difficult to control since the operation.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, moderately differentiated, head/uncinate process — as detailed above; ypT3 N1 M0, R0 resection, Status post open PPPD with en bloc SMV resection
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — previously diet-controlled; post-pancreatectomy diabetes now requiring pharmacologic management
3. Hypertension
4. Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency — new onset post-pancreatectomy, now on pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy
5. Eloxatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy, grade 1 (residual)
6. Post-operative pancreatic fistula (Grade B, ISGPF), resolved

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
1. ERCP with FCSEMS biliary stent placement (pre-treatment, for malignant biliary obstruction)
2. Open pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with en bloc SMV resection and primary venous reconstruction (approximately 10 weeks prior)

SOCIAL HISTORY
Retired librarian of Ghanaian descent. Lives with her husband. Has two adult children locally. Former smoker with a 15-pack-year history; quit approximately 10 years prior to diagnosis. No significant alcohol use. No illicit drug use. Good social support at home. Currently not working due to recovery.

FAMILY HISTORY
No known family history of pancreatic cancer. Family history of hypertension (mother). No known hereditary cancer syndromes. Germline testing (BRCA1/2, comprehensive panel) has been completed and was negative.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Pancrelipase (Creon) 72,000 lipase units with each meal, 36,000 lipase units with snacks
2. Metformin 500 mg twice daily (recently initiated post-pancreatectomy for worsened diabetes)
3. Insulin glargine (Lantus) — basal dose as titrated by endocrinology
4. Lisinopril 10 mg daily (hypertension)
5. Ondansetron 4 mg orally as needed for nausea (residual supply from chemoradiation)
6. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally as needed for nausea
7. Gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime (peripheral neuropathy)
8. Multivitamin daily
9. Vitamin D3 1000 IU daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Improved energy over the past 2-3 weeks. Reports regaining approximately 4 lbs since discharge. Appetite returning gradually. No fevers or chills.
Gastrointestinal: Stools have normalized in consistency with use of Creon. No overt steatorrhea at present. No nausea at rest; rare mild nausea with large meals. No vomiting. No abdominal pain. Drain site is well-healed.
Neurological: Persistent bilateral fingertip tingling and numbness, grade 1, stable. No new symptoms. No gait instability.
Endocrine: Blood glucose variability, currently managed with metformin and basal insulin in coordination with endocrinology. Fasting glucoses running 110-145 mg/dL per patient report. No hypoglycemic episodes.
Musculoskeletal: Generalized fatigue and mild deconditioning; no new joint pain.
Skin: Drain site scar healing well. No hand-foot syndrome at present (cape has been discontinued).
Cardiopulmonary: No chest pain, shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion, or edema.
Genitourinary: No dysuria, hematuria.
Hematologic: No unusual bleeding or bruising. No lymphadenopathy noted by patient.
Psychiatric: Reports some anxiety about cancer recurrence but no clinical depression. Engaged and asking informed questions. Strong family support.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, heart rate 72 bpm and regular, respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, temperature 98.2°F, oxygen saturation 98% on room air, weight 142 lbs (up from 138 lbs at last visit; pre-diagnosis weight approximately 156 lbs).

General: Well-appearing, pleasant 67-year-old woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Somewhat thin but improved from prior visits.

HEENT: Sclerae anicteric — notably improved from presentation. No conjunctival pallor. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis.

Neck: No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rhonchi, or crackles.

Abdomen: Well-healed midline laparotomy incision. Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly appreciated. No palpable masses. The drain site (right flank) is fully healed with a small residual scar. Bowel sounds present and normal.

Extremities: No lower extremity edema. Bilateral fingertip decreased sensation to light touch consistent with known neuropathy, symmetric, grade 1.

Skin: No rash, no hand-foot syndrome changes. No jaundice.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Normal gait. Strength 5/5 in all major muscle groups tested.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC: WBC 5.8 x10^3/mcL, hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from post-operative nadir), hematocrit 34.2%, platelets 198 x10^3/mcL.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, potassium 4.1 mEq/L, creatinine 0.82 mg/dL, BUN 16 mg/dL, total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, direct bilirubin 0.2 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, alkaline phosphatase 88 U/L, albumin 3.4 g/dL, total protein 6.8 g/dL.

Hemoglobin A1c: 7.4% (mildly elevated; will defer further adjustment to endocrinology).

Tumor Markers:
- CA 19-9: 18 U/mL (within normal limits; down from 52 U/mL pre-operatively, and from a peak of 1,204 U/mL at presentation)
- CEA: 2.4 ng/mL (within normal limits)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging was performed at this visit. The most recent imaging was the pre-operative CT abdomen/pelvis with pancreatic protocol and PET-CT obtained approximately 5 weeks prior to surgery, which showed the mass at 2.3 cm with SMA abutment reduced to less than 60 degrees, no hepatic lesions, no peritoneal disease, and no FDG-avid distant metastases. Post-operative surveillance CT is planned per protocol.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. PANCREATIC DUCTAL ADENOCARCINOMA — Status post neoadjuvant modified FOLFIRINOX x8 cycles (6 FOLFIRINOX, 2 FOLFIRI), concurrent chemoradiation (IMRT 54 Gy/28 fractions + cape), and open PPPD with en bloc SMV resection — R0 resection, ypT3 N1 M0. Currently approximately 10 weeks post-surgery. No clinical or biochemical evidence of recurrence at this time (CA 19-9 18 U/mL, CEA 2.4 ng/mL).

   Discussion of adjuvant chemotherapy:
   The patient has completed her neoadjuvant and surgical therapy with an R0 resection and has recovered adequately from her post-operative pancreatic fistula. The role of adjuvant chemotherapy following neoadjuvant therapy in pancreatic cancer is an active area of investigation. However, based on current best available evidence — including the ESPAC-4 trial, which demonstrated an overall survival benefit for Gemzar plus Xeloda (GemCap) over gem monotherapy in the adjuvant setting, with a median OS of 28 months versus 25.5 months and a 5-year OS rate of 28.8% versus 16.3% — adjuvant systemic chemotherapy is recommended. Although she has received prior fluoropyrimidine and Gemzar exposure in the curative-intent setting, the standard of care per ESPAC-4 and NCCN guidelines continues to support adjuvant GemCap for eligible patients following resection.

   KRAS G12V mutation status is noted from NGS; MRTX1133 and other KRAS G12D inhibitors are not applicable here. No other actionable targets are present. MSS status precludes benefit from immune checkpoint inhibition. The patient has been counseled regarding the evidence base.

   Plan: Initiate adjuvant Gemzar plus Xeloda (GemCap) at approximately 12 weeks post-surgery, targeting start in approximately 2 weeks pending lab recovery. Gemzar 1000 mg/m2 IV on days 1, 8, and 15 of each 28-day cycle; cape orally in divided doses on days 1-21 of each cycle. Given prior chemoradiation exposure, post-surgical debility, and residual neuropathy, Xeloda will be initiated at a reduced dose (approximately 80% of full dose, ~1330 mg/m2/day in divided doses) with plans to escalate if tolerated. Planned duration: 6 cycles (24 weeks). Labs will be obtained prior to each cycle. Anti-emetics and supportive care medications to be continued as needed.

   The patient is aware and agreeable to this plan. Goals, risks, and benefits were discussed, including the potential for hematologic toxicity, hand-foot syndrome (given prior cape exposure during chemoradiation), GI side effects, and the cumulative neuropathy risk with cape on background Eloxatin-induced neuropathy. She will be monitored closely for hand-foot syndrome given prior grade 1 palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia during chemoradiation.

2. EXOCRINE PANCREATIC INSUFFICIENCY — New onset post-pancreatectomy. Currently on pancrelipase (Creon) 72,000 lipase units with meals, 36,000 units with snacks. Patient reports improvement in stool consistency and reduction in greasy stools. Will continue current dosing. Referred to registered dietitian for ongoing nutritional optimization and guidance on fat intake and enzyme timing.

3. POST-PANCREATECTOMY DIABETES MELLITUS — Previously diet-controlled type 2 DM, now with worsened glycemic control following Whipple procedure (loss of islet cell mass). Currently managed with metformin and basal insulin glargine, being titrated by endocrinology. HbA1c 7.4%. Will continue co-management with endocrinology. Weight recovery is ongoing, which may also assist with glycemic control. Patient cautioned regarding risk of hypoglycemia with reduced oral intake during chemotherapy cycles.

4. Eloxatin-INDUCED PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY — Grade 1 (bilateral fingertip numbness/tingling), stable and not worsening over the past several weeks. Gabapentin 300 mg at bedtime continued for symptomatic management. No further Eloxatin is planned. Patient will be monitored for any exacerbation during adjuvant chemotherapy (Xeloda may have additive neurotoxic effects in some patients). If neuropathy worsens to grade 2 or higher, Xeloda dose adjustment will be considered.

5. NUTRITIONAL STATUS / WEIGHT RECOVERY — BMI currently reduced from pre-diagnosis baseline. Patient has regained 4 lbs since discharge and is trending upward. Encouraging higher caloric density meals and adequate protein. PERT optimized. Dietitian follow-up scheduled.

6. HYPERTENSION — Blood pressure well-controlled at 128/76 mmHg on lisinopril 10 mg daily. Continue current regimen. Will monitor renal function with initiation of chemotherapy.

7. PSYCHOSOCIAL — Patient expresses cancer-related anxiety, which is entirely expected given her diagnosis and treatment course. She has strong family and social support. She was provided with information regarding the pancreatic cancer survivorship clinic and cancer psychosocial support services. Social work referral offered; patient will consider.

FOLLOW-UP: Return in approximately 2 weeks for cycle 1 of adjuvant GemCap. Labs (CBC, CMP, LFTs) to be obtained 3-5 days prior to visit. CA 19-9 and CEA at start of adjuvant therapy. Surveillance CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast to be scheduled at approximately 3-4 months post-surgery as per NCCN pancreatic cancer surveillance guidelines. Patient given contact information for triage nurse for any concerns between visits.

Time spent with patient and family in counseling and coordination of care: approximately 55 minutes.

Electronically signed by: [Medical Oncology Attending]
Service: Medical Oncology / Pancreatic Cancer Program
